content.text: Overview

Observability data from a high-traffic GenAI service accumulates fast. At 1 000 requests per second, a service emits roughly 1 000 trace spans, 5 000 log events, and 200 metric scrape points every second. Without a retention policy, the cost of
storing everything forever
compounds until it consumes a meaningful fraction of the engineering budget.
A retention tier policy assigns each signal type and severity level to a
hot, warm, or cold tier
— each with a different cost/query-speed tradeoff and an explicit expiry. The policy answers: what do we keep, for how long, and why?

attrs.alt: Retention tier table — metrics, logs, traces, and prompt audit by tier and duration

content.text: The Three Storage Tiers
Tiers reflect two axes:
query speed
(how fast can an on-call engineer get results?) and
cost per GB per month
(how expensive is it to store?).
Tier
Storage medium
Query latency
Cost relative
Use case
Hot
SSD / in-memory (Prometheus TSDB, Loki ingester)
< 1 second
Active incident investigation; real-time dashboards
Warm
HDD / object store with query index (Thanos, Mimir, Loki with S3 backend)
1–30 seconds
Trend analysis; SLO burn history; post-mortem review
Cold
Object store only (S3 / GCS), no local index
30 s – 5 min
Capacity planning; regulatory; YoY cost reporting
The tier boundaries are architectural, not just configuration.
Hot tier data lives in Prometheus's local TSDB
or Loki's ingesters — in-memory or on local SSDs.
Warm tier data
lives in a long-term storage backend (Thanos object store, Loki's S3 chunks) with a query index that makes it searchable in seconds.
Cold tier data
is raw Parquet or compressed chunks in object storage with no query index — ad-hoc queries require a full scan.
Metric Retention Configuration
Prometheus handles hot-tier retention natively. Long-term metric retention requires Thanos or Grafana Mimir to write data to object store and serve queries across the extended window.
Prometheus (hot tier)

# prometheus/prometheus.yaml
global:
  scrape_interval: 15s
  evaluation_interval: 15s

# Retain 15 days of raw scrapes in local TSDB
# Beyond this, Thanos sidecar ships data to object store
storage:
  tsdb:
    retention.time: 15d
    retention.size: 50GB   # also cap by disk size
Thanos (warm + cold tiers)

# thanos/bucket-web.yaml — object store config
type: S3
config:
  bucket: prod-metrics-thanos
  endpoint: s3.us-east-1.amazonaws.com
  region: us-east-1

# thanos/compactor.yaml — downsampling and retention
compactor:
  # Downsample raw 15s data to 5m after 40 days
  # Downsample 5m data to 1h after 10 days of the 5m tier
  downsampling:
    disable: false

  retention:
    resolution_raw: 40d   # raw 15s scrapes
    resolution_5m:  90d   # 5-minute rollups
    resolution_1h:  730d  # 1-hour rollups (2 years)

The downsampling strategy is key: raw 15-second scrapes are expensive to store and rarely needed past 2 weeks. 5-minute rollups are sufficient for trend analysis. 1-hour rollups are sufficient for year-over-year capacity planning.
For GenAI cost metrics specifically,
preserve 1-hour rollups for 2 years
— finance teams regularly ask for quarterly and annual cost comparisons. Losing this data means reconstructing it from provider invoices.
Log Retention Configuration
Loki implements per-stream retention through its compactor. Retention periods are set by label selector — a stream labelled
level=debug
gets a different retention period than
level=error

# loki/config.yaml
limits_config:
  # Global default — all streams not matched by per-stream rules
  retention_period: 14d

  # Per-stream retention (Loki 2.8+)
  # Streams are matched by label selectors
  per_stream_rate_limit: 10MB
  per_stream_rate_limit_burst: 20MB

compactor:
  working_directory: /loki/compactor
  shared_store: s3
  compaction_interval: 10m
  retention_enabled: true
  retention_delete_delay: 2h      # time after marking before actual deletion
  retention_delete_worker_count: 150

# Per-stream retention rules (loki/retention_rules.yaml)
# Loaded by the compactor
retention_rules:
  - selector: '{level="debug"}'
    priority: 1
    period: 72h    # 3 days

  - selector: '{level="info"}'
    priority: 2
    period: 14d

  - selector: '{level="warning"}'
    priority: 3
    period: 30d

  - selector: '{level="error"}'
    priority: 4
    period: 90d

  - selector: '{service="genai-cost-monitor"}'
    priority: 5
    period: 365d   # cost attribution logs kept 1 year for finance
Trace Retention Configuration

Traces are the most voluminous signal per request (a single GenAI request may produce 20+ spans). Sampling is discussed in the next sub-subtopic; here the focus is on how long sampled traces are kept.
Grafana Tempo uses object storage for all traces and a query frontend that indexes by
trace_id
. Retention is set globally but can be overridden per tenant:

# tempo/tempo.yaml
compactor:
  compaction:
    block_retention: 7d     # sampled traces: 7 days hot

  ring:
    kvstore:
      store: memberlist

storage:
  trace:
    backend: s3
    s3:
      bucket: prod-traces-tempo
      region: us-east-1

# Error traces — longer retention via a separate tenant
# Use Tempo multi-tenancy: send error-sampled traces to tenant "error-traces"
# Configure that tenant with 30d retention
multitenancy_enabled: true
The 7-day retention for sampled traces is calibrated to the post-incident review window. Most incidents are investigated within 48 hours; 7 days provides a comfortable buffer. The
30-day retention for error traces
covers slow post-mortem timelines where a root cause investigation takes weeks.
Cost Estimation
Understanding the cost of each tier informs retention decisions. A rough model for a service processing 1 000 requests per second:

# Retention cost estimator for GenAI observability data
from dataclasses import dataclass

@dataclass
class RetentionCostEstimate:
    signal: str
    daily_gb: float
    retention_days: int
    cost_per_gb_per_month: float   # S3 = ~$0.023, hot SSD = ~$0.20

    @property
    def total_gb(self) -> float:
        return self.daily_gb * self.retention_days

    @property
    def monthly_cost_usd(self) -> float:
        return self.total_gb * self.cost_per_gb_per_month

# At 1000 req/s with 1% sampling for traces:
estimates = [
    RetentionCostEstimate("Metrics raw (15s)",       daily_gb=2.0,   retention_days=15,  cost_per_gb_per_month=0.20),
    RetentionCostEstimate("Metrics 5m rollup",       daily_gb=0.1,   retention_days=90,  cost_per_gb_per_month=0.023),
    RetentionCostEstimate("Metrics 1h rollup",       daily_gb=0.005, retention_days=730, cost_per_gb_per_month=0.023),
    RetentionCostEstimate("Logs debug (3d)",         daily_gb=10.0,  retention_days=3,   cost_per_gb_per_month=0.20),
    RetentionCostEstimate("Logs info (14d)",         daily_gb=5.0,   retention_days=14,  cost_per_gb_per_month=0.20),
    RetentionCostEstimate("Logs error (90d)",        daily_gb=0.5,   retention_days=90,  cost_per_gb_per_month=0.023),
    RetentionCostEstimate("Traces sampled 1% (7d)",  daily_gb=3.0,   retention_days=7,   cost_per_gb_per_month=0.20),
    RetentionCostEstimate("Traces errors (30d)",     daily_gb=0.3,   retention_days=30,  cost_per_gb_per_month=0.023),
]

print(f"{'Signal':<35} {'Total GB':>10} {'Monthly $':>12}")
print("-" * 60)
total = 0.0
for e in estimates:
    cost = e.monthly_cost_usd
    total += cost
    print(f"{e.signal:<35} {e.total_gb:>10.1f} {cost:>12.2f}")
print(f"{'TOTAL':<35} {'':>10} {total:>12.2f}")
# Example output (approximate):
# Metrics raw (15s)               30.0         6.00
# Metrics 5m rollup                9.0         0.21
# Metrics 1h rollup                3.7         0.08
# Logs debug (3d)                 30.0         6.00
# Logs info (14d)                 70.0        14.00
# Logs error (90d)                45.0         1.04
# Traces sampled 1% (7d)         21.0         4.20
# Traces errors (30d)             9.0          0.21
# TOTAL                                       31.74
The dominant cost driver is
info logs on hot-tier SSDs
— $14/month per 1 000 req/s even at a 14-day retention. Reducing info log verbosity or shifting them to a warm S3-backed tier after 3 days can cut this significantly.
Retention Policy as Code
Retention policies should live in the observability repository alongside alert rules and dashboard templates. A
retention.yaml
file documents the policy in human-readable form; CI validates that the deployed Loki/Thanos/Tempo configuration matches it:

# observability/retention.yaml  — source of truth
policy_version: "2025-Q4"
approved_by: alice-observability-lead
review_due: "2026-Q1"

metrics:
  raw_scrape:
    retention: 15d
    tier: hot
    backend: prometheus-tsdb
  rollup_5m:
    retention: 90d
    tier: warm
    backend: thanos-s3
  rollup_1h:
    retention: 2y
    tier: cold
    backend: thanos-s3

logs:
  debug:
    retention: 3d
    tier: hot
    label_selector: '{level="debug"}'
  info:
    retention: 14d
    tier: hot
    label_selector: '{level="info"}'
  warning:
    retention: 30d
    tier: warm
    label_selector: '{level="warning"}'
  error:
    retention: 90d
    tier: warm
    label_selector: '{level="error"}'
  cost_attribution:
    retention: 365d
    tier: warm
    label_selector: '{service="genai-cost-monitor"}'
    justification: "Finance requirement: annual cost audits"

traces:
  sampled:
    retention: 7d
    tier: hot
    backend: tempo
  error_traces:
    retention: 30d
    tier: warm
    backend: tempo-s3-tenant

prompt_audit:
  retention: contractual
  tier: cold
  backend: s3-separate-bucket
  access: restricted
  justification: "Safety review and regulatory compliance with explicit user consent"
Handling Retention Policy Changes
When shortening a retention period, data that already exists does not disappear immediately — it expires according to the
old
policy until the compactor processes it. When lengthening a retention period, data already deleted cannot be recovered.
Before changing any retention period:
Document the reason in the retention.yaml and get approval from the observability lead
For shortening: estimate cost savings and confirm no active investigation depends on the data being deleted
For lengthening: estimate the new storage cost and confirm budget approval
Deploy the config change during a low-traffic period to avoid compactor overload
Verify the change took effect by querying data at the old boundary — it should 404 after the compactor runs
Retention changes are
irreversible for data already deleted

. If you shorten error log retention from 90d to 30d and then need a trace from day 45 two weeks later, it is gone. When in doubt, keep data longer and re-evaluate storage costs quarterly rather than deleting aggressively.
Retention Review Cadence
Review the retention policy quarterly alongside the alert and dashboard reviews:
Check actual storage costs vs the estimate — if actual is 2× the estimate, investigate which stream is over-emitting
Review whether any new signal type (e.g. a new eval metric) is being retained under the default policy when it needs a custom one
Confirm that regulatory or contractual requirements have not changed (esp. for prompt audit retention)
Update the
review_due
date in
retention.yaml
and commit the updated policy to the repository
Summary

Retention tier policies balance query speed, storage cost, and operational need across three tiers: hot (SSD, seconds), warm (object store with index, seconds to minutes), and cold (raw object store, minutes).
Metrics: 15d raw on hot → 90d 5m rollup on warm → 2y 1h rollup on cold (Thanos compactor handles downsampling automatically)
Logs: 3d debug → 14d info → 30d warning → 90d error; cost attribution logs kept 1 year for finance
Traces: 7d sampled traces on hot → 30d error traces on warm; error traces in a separate Tempo tenant
Prompt audit logs live in cold storage on a separate S3 bucket with contractual retention and restricted access
Retention policy is checked into the observability repo as retention.yaml; CI validates deployed config matches
Retention changes require documented justification, cost analysis, and approver sign-off before deployment
